reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_150910_bf16_gemm_v1_7496aff2 at 24.537088 ms. This clean grouped-row experiment produced a useful negative result. Increasing kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 lowered barrier stalls from 5.46% to 5.33%, but long-scoreboard stalls rose from 7.24% to 7.33% and runtime regressed by 0.214 ms. That means the remaining gap to cuBLAS is not solved by deeper B-tile row grouping alone. The accepted clean base therefore stays at 24.324 ms, and the next move should target the long-scoreboard gap more directly while preserving the same row split and single-K stage depth. The best next clean experiment is the existing single-K 128x128 non-microkernel sibling, which changes the accumulate ordering without reopening the failed staged-128x128x32 path. The second choice is a launch-bounds retune to see whether the compiler can trim register pressure enough to help latency hiding. The third choice is the opposite grouped-row retune, from 4 down to 2, which tests whether the eight-row grouping overshot and whether tighter A locality is preferable to deeper B reuse.

family_audit.rationale: The grouped-row experiment only traded one stall class for another. The non-microkernel sibling is now the cleanest way to target long-scoreboard behavior while preserving the accepted row split and stage depth.
Launch-bounds retuning is still worth trying because the clean baseline remains latency-limited at 200 registers per thread, even if occupancy is not the whole answer.
The 8-row grouping was too deep. A smaller grouping depth is still a valid opposite-direction fallback, but it no longer deserves top priority.

directions.name: Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Non-Microkernel Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The grouped-row experiment showed that launch-order changes alone are not enough. The existing single-K 128x128 sibling keeps the accepted row split and one-K stage depth while changing the accumulate ordering away from the microkernel-specific path, which may reduce long-scoreboard waste directly.
directions.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is contributing to long-scoreboard stalls on the accepted hot-band split
directions.risk: Moderate risk: the variant is in-tree, but it still lacks a clean measurement on the accepted row split.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest next experiment that directly targets the long-scoreboard gap exposed by the grouped-row result.
It preserves the accepted hot-band row split, so the measurement stays focused on one kernel-variant change.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again while long-scoreboard stalls stay flat or worsen.
expected_local_changes: Restore the grouped-row constant back to the clean accepted base if needed.
Swap the default hot-band launch from the PTX microkernel path to the existing single-K non-microkernel 128x128 sibling.

directions.name: Retune PTX Launch Bounds On The Clean Baseline
directions.hypothesis: The clean PTX hotspot still sits at 200 registers per thread with occupancy capped at two blocks per SM. Tightening __launch_bounds__ may trim enough register pressure to improve latency hiding if the compiler does not spill.
directions.expected_bottleneck: register pressure and low CTA residency on the accepted PTX hot-band path
directions.risk: High risk: forcing tighter launch bounds can introduce spills or a worse schedule, and the clean cuBLAS reference does not point to occupancy as the main missing ingredient.
ranking_notes: This remains worth keeping because the clean PTX path is still latency-limited.
It ranks behind the non-microkernel sibling because the grouped-row result points more strongly at scoreboard behavior than at occupancy.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if build output or NCU shows spills, or if runtime regresses without lowering register pressure.
expected_local_changes: Restore the grouped-row constant back to the clean accepted base if needed.
Retune the PTX microkernel launch-bounds annotation and keep the rest of the launch topology unchanged for the first measurement.

directions.name: Try A Shallower PTX Grouped-Row Setting
directions.hypothesis: The failed 8-row grouping suggests the PTX hot-band path may prefer tighter A locality over deeper B reuse. Dropping kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 2 tests the opposite launch-order direction without changing the row split or K-stage structure.
directions.expected_bottleneck: current grouped-row traversal may still be mismatched to A/B locality balance on the accepted PTX hot-band path
directions.risk: Moderate risk: this is the same family that just failed in the opposite direction, so the upside may be small.
ranking_notes: This stays in reserve as the opposite-direction launch-order test after the 8-row setting failed.
It ranks third because the non-microkernel sibling is now the more direct scoreboard experiment.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within noise or if scoreboard stalls do not improve after one measurement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the grouped-row constant back to the clean accepted base if needed.
Decrease kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 2 and remeasure.

notes: This diagnosis continues the clean 10-round loop. The 8-row grouped setting is now treated as a measured negative result, so the next recommended clean round moves to the non-microkernel sibling.
